body_txt: The archetypical pet for Shrinking Violet or loner characters is the domestic cat. An introverted character will often also be a Kindhearted Cat Lover . Oftentimes the cat(s) are a replacement for human interaction , or in the case of lonely single women, lovers, and occasionally children. The popularity of this trope likely occurs for a few different reasons: Cats, in contrast with dogs, are generally an inside pet. They don't require walks and can live their entire lives indoors. As a result, they're great for people who dislike going outside. Being solitary hunters in the wild rather than pack animals, cats are also inherently less socially-oriented compared to dogs, and tend to incidentally "respect" the boundaries of an introverted owner more; even the clingiest and most affectionate cat might hop up on their human and lick their face only occasionally when they're particularly content, compared to an affectionate but undisciplined dog that will do the same thing at every possible opportunity , behavior which an introvert is likely to get annoyed with in short order. There's also the matter that cats and introverts are both commonly misunderstood by those who don't bother trying to meet them at their own level ; cats can be every bit as sweet-natured and loving as dogs, but someone who hasn't learned their unique communicative behaviors and just expects them to match up 1:1 with those of a dog would not know this and might simply conclude Cats Are Mean (yes, much like how too many outgoing people mistakenly assume introverts are at best defective extroverts in need of "fixing", or rude, distant jerkasses at worst). Based on their usual lived experience in dealing with others who don't "get" them, introverts are a bit more likely to put in the effort of learning their cat's idiosyncrasies and ways of communicating instead of resenting it for not being able to easily comprehend their own, which makes for a much happier and more affectionate cat that defies the stock "cruel, pompous, indifferent jerk" stereotype pop culture has saddled them with. This also applies to other pets such as birds, small rodents, and reptiles, however those rarely appear in fiction (and when they do they're usually owned by "weird" characters ). This person might get called a Crazy Cat Lady and might develop into one as they get older, but the difference is that a Crazy Cat Lady has a mental illness, while (most) Introverted Cat People do not. The feline counterpart of Canine Companion . Contrast with Heroes Love Dogs , which this trope tends to be a foil to (with more extroverted characters preferring dogs). Related to Right-Hand Cat if the evil boss is Lonely at the Top . Super-Trope to Crazy Cat Lady .
